title: Setup & First Script
objective: Install Python/Jupyter, run your first .py file and notebook cell.
concepts: Running cells in JupyterLab or VS Code
exercise: Write a script that prints a greeting, your name, and today's date using datetime.date.today()
homework: Write 5 bullet points on why Python is used in data/ML.

title: Basic Types & Variables
objective: Practice int, float, str, bool and variable assignment.
concepts: Basic types: int, float, str, bool
type() to inspect a variable's type
exercise: Create variables for your age, height, name, and a boolean. Print them in a friendly sentence. Compute and print (100 + 50) * 2 / 3
homework: Describe the difference between int and float in your own words with one example each. Tuesday

title: if/else and Conditionals
objective: Control program flow with if/elif/else.
exercise: Write a BMI category classifier: underweight, normal, overweight, obese. Given a score (0–100), print the letter grade A/B/C/D/F.
homework: Explain why indentation matters in Python. Write an example that would break without correct indentation.

title: for Loops & range()
objective: Repeat actions with for loops and range().
exercise: Implement FizzBuzz from 1 to 50. Sum all even numbers from 1 to 100 using a loop.
homework: What is the difference between range(5) and range(1,6)? Show with code. Wednesday

title: Defining Functions
objective: Define and call reusable functions with parameters and return values.
concepts: Calling functions with positional vs keyword args
exercise: Define compute_bmi(weight_kg, height_m) and test on 3 people. Write a greet(name, greeting='Hello') function.
homework: Write a function that takes a list of numbers and returns (min, max, average) as a tuple.

title: Modules & the math Library
objective: Import and use standard library modules, starting with math.
concepts: import module and import module as alias
Using help() and dir() to explore modules
exercise: Write circle_area(r) and sphere_volume(r) using math.pi. Compute the hypotenuse of a right triangle with sides 3 and 4.
homework: Explore the random module: generate 5 random integers between 1 and 10. Thursday

title: Lists & Basic Operations
objective: Create and manipulate Python lists for storing sequences of data.
exercise: Store daily step counts for 7 days. Compute total and average. Given a list of marks, remove the lowest mark and compute the new average.
homework: What is the difference between list.append(x) and list.insert(0, x)? Give examples.

title: Dictionaries for Structured Data
objective: Use dicts to store labeled, key-value data.
exercise: Represent 3 students as dicts with name and marks. Compute class average. Build a word-frequency counter for a short sentence.
homework: Describe a real-world situation where a dictionary is more useful than a list. Friday

title: Using help(), dir() & Reading Docs
objective: Become confident exploring Python from the inside.
concepts: type() and isinstance() for type checking
exercise: Use help() on len, list.sort, str.split. Write a 1-line note + example for each. Use dir([]) to find and test 3 list methods you haven't used yet.
homework: Find the signature and behavior of str.join() from the docs and write a practical example.

title: Mini-Project: Personal Stats Summary
objective: Integrate variables, lists, loops, and functions into one script.
concepts: Combining building blocks into a real program
exercise: Build a script: input 7 days of (steps, study_hours, water_liters), compute totals and averages, print a clear formatted report. Add a function highlight(value, goal) that prints 'Great!' or 'Try harder!' depending on whether the goal was met.
homework: Reflection: list 3 things you learned, 2 things that confused you, 1 thing you want to explore further.

title: NumPy Intro & Array Creation
objective: Install NumPy and understand why it's faster than Python lists.
concepts: Comparing list operations vs vectorized NumPy
ndarray properties: shape, ndim, dtype, size
exercise: Convert [1,2,3,4,5] to an array. Print shape, ndim, dtype. Compare speed: sum a list of 1 million numbers with Python vs NumPy (use time module).
homework: Explain broadcasting in your own words (even a rough first idea). We'll verify it later.

title: Array Creation Helpers & Shapes
objective: Use factory functions to create arrays of any shape quickly.
exercise: Create a 3×4 matrix of zeros, print shape, then reshape to 2×6. Create an array of 10 evenly spaced values between 0 and 1 using linspace.
homework: Write a 'multiplication table' as a 10×10 NumPy array using np.arange and reshape. Tuesday

title: Indexing & Slicing (1D)
objective: Select individual elements and sub-arrays from 1D arrays.
exercise: From np.arange(100), extract: first 10 elements, last 5, every other element. Reverse an array using slicing.
homework: Write a function first_n_evens(arr, n) that returns the first n even-indexed elements.

title: Indexing & Slicing (2D)
objective: Select rows, columns, and submatrices from 2D arrays.
concepts: arr[row, :] for full row; arr[:, col] for full column
exercise: From a 5×5 matrix (1–25), extract: row 2, column 3, top-left 3×3 block. Replace the entire diagonal of a 4×4 matrix with zeros.
homework: Create a checkerboard 4×4 0/1 pattern without explicit loops. Wednesday

title: Boolean Masks & Fancy Indexing
objective: Filter arrays using conditions; use index arrays.
concepts: Boolean condition produces a mask: arr > 5
exercise: From 0–99, extract: values between 20–50, values NOT divisible by 3. Replace all values below 10 with 0 in a random array.
homework: Use np.where to create a new array where positive values stay, negative become -1.

title: Vectorized Operations & Broadcasting
objective: Apply operations to entire arrays without loops.
exercise: Convert an array of Celsius temps to Fahrenheit vectorized. Given a 3×4 matrix of exam scores, subtract each student's mean (row-wise) using broadcasting.
homework: Draw (or write) the shape diagram for adding a (3,1) array to a (1,4) array. What shape results? Thursday

title: Aggregations & Statistics
objective: Summarize arrays with aggregate functions along any axis.
concepts: sum(), mean(), std(), var(), min(), max()
exercise: For a 4×5 random matrix: compute max per row, min per column, overall std. Given daily temperatures for 4 weeks (4×7), compute weekly averages.
homework: Explain the difference between axis=0 and axis=1 with a diagram or example.

title: Linear Algebra Basics
objective: Perform matrix operations used frequently in ML.
concepts: Matrix multiplication: A @ B or np.dot(A, B)
Solving a linear system: np.linalg.solve(A, b)
exercise: Create A (2×3) and B (3×2), compute A @ B, verify shape. Solve the system 2x + y = 5, x - y = 1 using np.linalg.solve.
homework: Verify that A @ np.linalg.inv(A) ≈ identity matrix for a 3×3 matrix. Friday

title: Random Numbers & Simulation
objective: Generate reproducible random data for simulations and ML.
concepts: Generating from distributions: normal, uniform, Poisson
exercise: Simulate 10,000 dice rolls; count and print frequency of each face. Generate 1000 samples from N(0,1); verify mean ≈ 0 and std ≈ 1.
homework: Write a Monte Carlo estimate of π using random points in a unit square.

title: Mini-Project: NumPy Numeric Simulation
objective: Combine arrays, broadcasting, and aggregations in one project.
concepts: Combining indexing, stats, and random data
exercise: Simulate 10,000 coin flips using np.random.randint(0,2). Count heads and tails. Compute running proportion of heads and find when it first settles within 1% of 0.5. Bonus: Print a text bar chart of the frequency of each outcome.
homework: Reflection: Write your personal NumPy cheat sheet — the 10 most useful operations you learned.

title: Series & DataFrame Basics
objective: Create Series and DataFrames from scratch and understand their structure.
concepts: pd.DataFrame from dict of lists or list of dicts
exercise: Create a DataFrame of 5 cities with population, area, and country columns. Print the dtype of each column and describe() the numeric ones.
homework: Compare a pandas Series to a NumPy array — what are 2 differences and 1 similarity?

title: Loading & Inspecting Data
objective: Read real datasets from CSV and other formats.
exercise: Load the Iris CSV dataset, print head(5), shape, and column dtypes. Check how many rows have a sepal_length > 5.0.
homework: Find one open dataset on Kaggle or GitHub, load it, and write 3 observations from describe(). Tuesday

title: Selecting Rows & Columns
objective: Access subsets of a DataFrame with loc, iloc, and column selection.
exercise: Select only numeric columns from the Iris dataset. Use loc to get rows where species == 'setosa' and only the petal columns.
homework: What is the key difference between loc and iloc? When would each be confusing?

title: Filtering, Sorting & Value Counts
objective: Slice and sort DataFrames to answer real questions.
exercise: Filter rows where sepal_length > 5 AND petal_width < 2. Sort by sepal_length descending. Find the 5 most common species in a dataset using value_counts.
homework: Create a 'filtered_data.csv' of the top 20 rows by sepal_length. Wednesday

title: Handling Missing Data
objective: Detect and handle NaN values appropriately.
concepts: Percentage of missing values per column
exercise: Create a DataFrame with deliberate NaNs. Compare dropna() vs fillna(0) vs fillna(mean). Write a function missing_report(df) that prints % missing per column.
homework: When would you prefer fillna(mean) vs dropna()? Give a real scenario for each.

title: Type Conversion & New Columns
objective: Fix dtypes and engineer new features from existing columns.
concepts: apply() with lambda for row/column transforms
exercise: Add a BMI column to a health dataset using weight and height columns. Convert a 'date' string column to datetime and extract year, month, day.
homework: Write an apply() function that categorizes a numeric column into 'low', 'medium', 'high' bins. Thursday

title: GroupBy & Aggregations
objective: Summarize data by category using groupby.
concepts: Multiple aggregation functions: mean, sum, count, std
exercise: Using the tips dataset (seaborn), compute average tip by day and by smoker status. Find the top 3 days by total bill amount.
homework: Compare groupby().mean() with a manual loop approach — what are the advantages?

title: Merge, Join & Concat
objective: Combine multiple DataFrames together.
concepts: pd.merge() with how: inner, left, right, outer
merge on: single key, multiple keys
Handling duplicate column names after merge
exercise: Create two small DataFrames (students and their scores) and merge them on 'student_id'. Stack three DataFrames of monthly sales data using pd.concat.
homework: Describe the difference between inner and left join with a 5-row example. Friday

title: Exporting & I/O Operations
objective: Save processed DataFrames to various file formats.
concepts: index=False to avoid extra index column
exercise: Load a CSV, clean it (drop nulls, fix types), and export cleaned version with a new filename. Export a summary groupby result to Excel with a named sheet.
homework: What's the risk of saving index=True when re-loading a CSV later? Test it.

title: Mini-Project: Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
objective: Perform a full EDA on a real dataset using pandas only.
concepts: Combining load, clean, filter, groupby, export
Writing markdown insight notes in a notebook
exercise: Choose a dataset (tips, flights, or titanic from seaborn). Load → clean → 5 groupby insights → export cleaned CSV → write 5 markdown bullet 'findings'. Identify the single most interesting pattern in your data and explain why.
homework: Reflection: Write a 'pandas cheat sheet' with the 12 operations you used most this week.

title: Matplotlib: Figures & Axes
objective: Understand the Figure/Axes hierarchy and create basic line plots.
exercise: Plot daily temperatures for 7 days as a line chart with labeled axes and title. Plot two overlapping lines on the same axes with a legend.
homework: Explain the difference between plt.plot() and fig, ax = plt.subplots(); ax.plot().

title: Common Plot Types
objective: Create bar, scatter, and histogram charts.
concepts: ax.bar() and ax.barh() for bar charts
Color, alpha, and edge color options
exercise: Bar chart of average tip by day (from tips dataset). Histogram of sepal_length with 20 bins.
homework: When would you choose a histogram over a scatter plot? Give a real scenario. Tuesday

title: Plot Customization & Styling
objective: Make plots professional and readable.
exercise: Recreate yesterday's bar chart with improved styling: custom colors, rotated x labels, grid lines. Save your best plot as a PNG at 150 DPI.
homework: Find the 3 matplotlib styles you like best by running plt.style.available and comparing them.

title: Subplots & Layout
objective: Display multiple plots in a single figure.
exercise: Create a 1×2 subplot: histogram on left, scatter on right for the same dataset. Create a 2×2 grid of 4 different chart types for the Iris dataset.
homework: Explain when to use subplots vs creating separate figures. Wednesday

title: Seaborn: Introduction & Distribution Plots
objective: Use Seaborn for statistically-informed, beautiful visualizations.
exercise: sns.histplot of sepal_length colored by species. Overlay a KDE on the histogram.
homework: What does KDE (Kernel Density Estimate) represent? Explain in plain language.

title: Seaborn: Scatter, Regression & Pair Plots
objective: Visualize relationships between variables.
exercise: Scatter plot of sepal_length vs petal_length, colored by species. sns.pairplot on the full Iris dataset.
homework: From pairplot, identify the two variables with the strongest visual correlation. Why? Thursday

title: Seaborn: Categorical & Statistical Plots
objective: Visualize distributions and comparisons across groups.
exercise: Boxplot of tip amount grouped by day of week. Violinplot comparing petal_length by species.
homework: Describe what a violin plot shows that a boxplot doesn't. When is each more useful?

title: Heatmaps & Correlation Matrices
objective: Visualize multi-variable relationships in a grid.
exercise: Create an annotated heatmap of the Iris correlation matrix. Mask the upper triangle of the heatmap for a cleaner look.
homework: From the heatmap, which 2 features are most positively correlated? Does it make physical sense? Friday

title: Interactive Visualizations with Plotly (Intro)
objective: Create interactive charts that users can zoom and hover.
exercise: Recreate the Iris scatter plot as an interactive Plotly chart with species hover info. Export it as an HTML file you can open in a browser.
homework: Compare Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly: when would you choose each?

title: Mini-Project: Visual Data Story
objective: Build a complete EDA notebook with 4+ plots telling a story.
concepts: Selecting the right chart type for the question
exercise: Using the tips or titanic dataset: create (1) a distribution plot, (2) a relationship plot, (3) a category comparison, and (4) a correlation heatmap. Add a 1-sentence insight below each. Write a 'headline finding' as the first cell in your notebook.
homework: Reflection: List the 5 most powerful visualization techniques you learned this week and when to use each.

title: CSV & JSON with Standard Library
objective: Read/write CSV and JSON using Python's built-in modules.
exercise: Write a list of student dicts to CSV and read it back with csv.DictReader. Save a dict as pretty-printed JSON and reload it.
homework: When would you use the csv module vs pandas read_csv? Give 2 scenarios for each.

title: Excel with pandas & openpyxl
objective: Read and write Excel files with multiple sheets.
exercise: Load an Excel file with 2 sheets; combine them into one DataFrame. Export a cleaned DataFrame to Excel with a custom sheet name.
homework: What are the practical differences between CSV and Excel for data storage? Tuesday

title: XML Parsing
objective: Parse structured XML data using Python's xml.etree module.
exercise: Parse a small XML file (e.g., RSS feed or sample data) and extract all title and link elements. Convert extracted XML data to a pandas DataFrame.
homework: Compare XML and JSON as data formats: pros and cons of each.

title: Text & Log File Parsing
objective: Extract structured data from plain text and log files.
concepts: Building a list of parsed records
exercise: Read a log file; count ERROR, WARNING, and INFO lines separately. Extract timestamps and messages from each line into a DataFrame.
homework: Write a function parse_log(filepath) → DataFrame that generalizes this pattern. Wednesday

title: Regex for Data Cleaning
objective: Use regular expressions to find and transform patterns in text.
exercise: Extract all email addresses from a text string. Normalize phone numbers to (XXX) XXX-XXXX format from messy input.
homework: Write a regex that extracts dates in MM/DD/YYYY format from a paragraph.

title: PDF Reading with pdfplumber
objective: Extract text and tables from PDF files programmatically.
exercise: Open a PDF, extract text from page 1, and print the first 500 characters. Extract a table from a PDF and convert it to a pandas DataFrame.
homework: What are the challenges of PDF parsing? Why do tables in PDFs cause problems? Thursday

title: Image Basics with Pillow
objective: Open, inspect, and transform images programmatically.
exercise: Open an image, print its size and mode, create a thumbnail, save as PNG. Convert an RGB image to grayscale.
homework: What does img.mode tell you? What is the difference between 'RGB', 'L', and 'RGBA'?

title: Building a Data Cleaning Pipeline
objective: Combine file I/O, regex, and pandas into a reusable pipeline.
concepts: Pipeline pattern: load → validate → clean → export
Using functions to modularize each step
Logging intermediate results with print or logging module
exercise: Build a pipeline: read messy CSV → strip whitespace and fix casing → fill missing values → export cleaned CSV with a timestamp in the filename. Add a function that generates a short cleaning report (rows before/after, nulls removed).
homework: Sketch the pipeline as a flowchart (even on paper). Identify where errors are most likely to occur. Friday

title: Data Merging from Multiple Sources
objective: Combine data from CSV, JSON, and Excel into one unified DataFrame.
concepts: Loading from different formats into DataFrames
exercise: Load the same dataset stored as CSV, JSON, and Excel. Merge all three and verify no duplicates. Write a function load_and_merge(file_list) that handles CSV, JSON, and Excel automatically.
homework: What real-world problems arise when merging data from multiple sources? List 3 and how to handle them.

title: Mini-Project: Data Cleaning Notebook
objective: Create a professional data cleaning notebook from raw to clean data.
concepts: Outputting a clean CSV and a cleaning report
exercise: Take a messy dataset (missing values, wrong types, duplicate rows, inconsistent casing). Clean it step by step with explanations. Produce cleaned_data.csv and a markdown 'cleaning log' that describes each step. Bonus: visualize before/after statistics for 2 columns.
homework: Reflection: Write your 'data cleaning checklist' — the 8 steps you'll follow every time.

title: HTTP Basics & GET Requests
objective: Understand HTTP and make your first API call with requests.
concepts: HTTP concepts: URL, method, status codes, headers
exercise: Call a public API (e.g., Open-Notify ISS location or Random User). Print the JSON response. Print the status code and confirm it's 200 before using the data.
homework: What is the difference between a 200, 404, and 500 status code? Give a real-world analogy.

title: Query Parameters & Headers
objective: Pass data to APIs using query parameters and request headers.
concepts: headers dict for Auth, Content-Type, User-Agent
exercise: Call a search API (e.g., Open Library or REST Countries) with a query parameter. Print number of results. Write a loop that fetches 3 pages of paginated results and collects all records.
homework: Why might an API return different results for the same URL without vs with a User-Agent header? Tuesday

title: Parsing JSON into pandas
objective: Convert API JSON responses into DataFrames for analysis.
exercise: Fetch 50 random user records from randomuser.me. Convert to a DataFrame with name, email, country. Normalize a nested JSON field (e.g., coordinates) into separate columns.
homework: What challenges arise with deeply nested JSON? How does json_normalize help?

title: Rate Limiting & Robustness
objective: Write production-quality API fetchers that handle errors and limits.
concepts: Retry logic with a simple loop
Saving raw JSON to file as backup
exercise: Write a function fetch_with_retry(url, retries=3) that retries on 5xx errors. Fetch 10 pages of data with a 1-second sleep between each; collect all results.
homework: Why is it important to save raw API data before processing it? Describe a scenario where this matters. Wednesday

title: BeautifulSoup: HTML Parsing Basics
objective: Parse and navigate HTML documents to extract data.
concepts: find() and find_all() by tag and attribute
exercise: Fetch a static web page and print all <h2> headings. Extract all hyperlinks from a page and store in a list.
homework: What is the difference between find() and find_all()? When does each return None?

title: Scraping Tables & Building DataFrames
objective: Turn HTML tables into pandas DataFrames.
concepts: Manual row/cell extraction for complex tables
Cleaning scraped data: strip, replace, convert types
exercise: Use pd.read_html() to extract a table from a Wikipedia page. Manually parse a table row-by-row with BeautifulSoup and build a DataFrame.
homework: pd.read_html() is convenient but sometimes wrong. When would you prefer manual parsing? Thursday

title: Ethical Scraping & robots.txt
objective: Understand the legal and ethical boundaries of web scraping.
concepts: Reading robots.txt to understand allowed/disallowed paths
Terms of Service and public vs private data
exercise: Fetch and print the robots.txt for 3 websites you use. Identify which paths are disallowed and which are allowed for scrapers.
homework: Write a 150-word reflection: When is web scraping ethical? What lines should never be crossed?

title: Saving & Processing Scraped Data
objective: Clean and store scraped data for downstream analysis.
concepts: Stripping whitespace and special characters from scraped text
Handling missing cells in scraped tables
Converting text numbers to numeric types
Saving to CSV and back-dating raw HTML
exercise: Scrape a simple sports or financial table. Clean it and compute one meaningful stat (e.g., average). Save both the raw HTML and the cleaned CSV with timestamps.
homework: What encoding issues can arise in scraped text? How does .encode('utf-8', errors='ignore') help? Friday

title: API + pandas Mini-Dashboard
objective: Build a small pipeline from API → DataFrame → charts.
concepts: Creating a summary table and 2 plots from live data
exercise: Fetch weather data or COVID stats from a public API. Build a DataFrame. Plot daily trend and a bar chart of top 5 values. Export the report as a CSV + two PNG charts.
homework: What improvements would make this pipeline 'production-ready'? List 3 enhancements.

title: Mini-Project: API-Based Mini Dataset
objective: Complete end-to-end project: fetch → clean → analyze → visualize.
concepts: Designing a project with a clear question
exercise: Choose one public API (e.g., OpenWeatherMap, REST Countries, NASA APOD). Fetch at least 50 records. Clean, store to CSV, compute 3 statistics, and produce 2 charts. Write a 3-sentence 'finding' section in your notebook. Bonus: wrap your entire pipeline into one function run_pipeline() that can be re-run.
homework: Reflection: List 3 real-world datasets you could analyze with APIs + pandas that would be personally meaningful to you.

title: Flask: Getting Started
objective: Build and run your first Flask web application.
exercise: Create a Flask app with routes: /, /hello/<name>, /about. Test in browser. Return a JSON response from a /data route.
homework: What does debug=True do? Why should it NEVER be used in production?

title: Flask: Templates & Jinja2
objective: Render dynamic HTML pages using Jinja2 templates.
exercise: Create a template that displays a list of items passed from Flask. Add a base.html template with a navbar and extend it in 2 other pages.
homework: How does Jinja2 template inheritance reduce code duplication? Give a concrete example. Tuesday

title: Flask: Forms & POST Requests
objective: Handle user input from HTML forms.
exercise: Build a BMI calculator: HTML form takes weight and height, Flask computes and displays BMI. Add basic validation: check that values are numeric before computing.
homework: What is the POST/Redirect/GET pattern and why does it prevent form resubmission?

title: Flask: REST API Endpoints
objective: Build a JSON REST API with Flask.
concepts: HTTP methods: GET, POST, DELETE on same route with methods=['GET','POST']
exercise: Build a simple in-memory 'todo list' API: GET /todos, POST /todos, DELETE /todos/<id>. Test your API using the requests library or a browser.
homework: What is REST? List its 5 key constraints in your own words. Wednesday

title: Flask: Serving Data from pandas
objective: Connect Flask to a pandas dataset and serve computed results.
concepts: Loading a CSV once at startup with global variable
Returning filtered data as JSON from API endpoint
exercise: Build a Flask app that loads the Iris dataset and serves: /data (JSON all), /stats (JSON describe), /species/<name> (filtered rows). Render a simple HTML table of the first 10 rows via a template.
homework: What is the risk of using a global DataFrame in Flask? How could you improve this?

title: FastAPI: Modern API Design
objective: Build a type-safe, auto-documented API with FastAPI.
concepts: Path parameters and query parameters with type hints
exercise: Create a FastAPI app with 3 routes: /, /items/{item_id}, /search?q=term. Test in /docs. Add a query parameter with a default value and an optional description.
homework: Compare Flask and FastAPI: what are the 3 biggest differences for a beginner building an API? Thursday

title: FastAPI: Pydantic Models & Validation
objective: Use Pydantic models to validate request and response data automatically.
concepts: Defining request body models with type annotations
exercise: Define a Student model with name (str), age (int, min 0), grade (float, 0–100). POST endpoint that returns the validated student. Test with invalid data and observe FastAPI's automatic error responses.
homework: What is the advantage of Pydantic validation over manually checking request data in Flask?

title: FastAPI: Async Support & Background Tasks
objective: Understand async endpoints and simple background tasks.
concepts: async def vs def in FastAPI
When async improves performance (I/O bound tasks)
exercise: Convert a FastAPI endpoint to async and add a 'log to file' background task. Compare response time with and without async for a simulated I/O operation.
homework: Explain the event loop concept in 3 sentences suitable for a beginner. Friday

title: Wrapping ML Models as APIs
objective: Serve a pre-trained ML model as an API endpoint.
concepts: Loading a saved model (joblib or pickle)
Accepting input data via Pydantic model
exercise: Train a simple Iris classifier with scikit-learn, save it, and serve predictions via a FastAPI endpoint that accepts sepal/petal measurements. Test it with valid and invalid inputs.
homework: What additional concerns arise when deploying an ML model as an API vs a static data API?

title: Mini-Project: Data API with Flask or FastAPI
objective: Build a complete small API that exposes a dataset with filtering and stats.
exercise: Build a /population API (use rest countries or similar): GET all countries, GET /country/<name> (details), GET /stats (global averages), GET /filter?region=Europe. Document all endpoints. Bonus: add a /predict endpoint if you already have a trained model.
homework: Reflection: Sketch on paper the architecture of a real-world data application using Flask/FastAPI + pandas + a database. Label each component.

title: ML Workflow & train-test split
objective: Understand the end-to-end supervised learning workflow.
concepts: Scikit-learn estimator API: fit(), predict(), score()
exercise: Load Iris, split 80/20, confirm shapes. Fit a KNN classifier. Print test accuracy. Try 3 different random_state values and observe how accuracy changes.
homework: What is data leakage? Give a concrete example of how it happens and why it's catastrophic.

title: Classification: KNN & Logistic Regression
objective: Train and evaluate two classification algorithms.
exercise: Compare KNN and Logistic Regression on the Iris dataset. Which is more accurate? Try k=1, 5, 15 for KNN. Plot accuracy vs k.
homework: What is the bias-variance trade-off? How does k in KNN relate to it? Tuesday

title: Classification: Decision Trees & Random Forests
objective: Use tree-based models for classification.
concepts: Overfitting in trees and how depth controls it
exercise: Train a Decision Tree (depth=3) and a Random Forest (100 trees) on a dataset. Compare accuracy. Plot feature importances from the Random Forest as a bar chart.
homework: Why does a single deep decision tree overfit but a Random Forest doesn't?

title: Regression: Linear & Polynomial
objective: Predict continuous outcomes with regression models.
concepts: Mean Squared Error (MSE) and R² score
Visualizing the fit line vs data points
exercise: Train a Linear Regression on a housing price dataset (or Boston/diabetes). Compute RMSE and R². Add PolynomialFeatures (degree=2) and compare R² before/after.
homework: When does a high R² indicate a good model? When can it be misleading? Wednesday

title: Preprocessing: Scaling & Encoding
objective: Prepare raw features for machine learning models.
concepts: Why scaling matters for KNN, SVM but not trees
exercise: Apply StandardScaler to features and compare KNN accuracy before/after scaling. One-hot encode a categorical column and verify no information leak into test set.
homework: Name 3 algorithms that require feature scaling and 3 that do not. Explain why.

title: Pipelines: Preventing Data Leakage
objective: Build sklearn Pipelines to combine preprocessing and model training safely.
concepts: Fit pipeline on train set only
exercise: Build a Pipeline: StandardScaler + Logistic Regression. Fit on train, evaluate on test. Build a Pipeline for a dataset with both numeric and categorical features using ColumnTransformer.
homework: Explain why fitting a scaler on the entire dataset (before splitting) is a form of data leakage. Thursday

title: Evaluation Metrics (Classification)
objective: Go beyond accuracy to fully evaluate classification models.
concepts: Confusion matrix: TP, TN, FP, FN
ROC curve and AUC for binary classification
exercise: Evaluate a classifier with classification_report and a confusion matrix heatmap. Plot the ROC curve for a binary classification problem.
homework: Explain precision vs recall in plain English using a spam detection or cancer detection example.

title: Cross-Validation & Hyperparameter Tuning
objective: Use cross-validation for reliable estimates and grid search to tune models.
concepts: Why single train/test split can be misleading
exercise: Use 5-fold cross-validation on 3 models. Compare mean and std of scores. Run GridSearchCV on KNN over n_neighbors=[1,3,5,7,9]. Print best k and accuracy.
homework: When would you prefer RandomizedSearchCV over GridSearchCV? Give a practical example. Friday

title: Saving & Loading Models
objective: Persist trained models for later use without retraining.
exercise: Train a model, save it with joblib. Load it in a new script and make predictions. Verify results match. Save and load a full Pipeline (scaler + model) and verify predictions are identical.
homework: What information besides the model file do you need to save to reproduce a deployment? List 5 things.

title: Mini-Project: Complete ML Notebook
objective: Build a full supervised learning project notebook.
concepts: Problem definition → data → preprocessing → modeling → evaluation → insights
exercise: Choose a dataset (Titanic, diabetes, or similar). Perform: EDA (3 plots) → preprocessing (Pipeline) → train 3 models → evaluate with cross-validation → report best model with all metrics. Write a 'model card' cell: describe what the model does, its limitations, and how it should be used.
homework: Reflection: Write your ML project checklist — the 10 steps you'll always follow.

title: SciPy Overview & Statistics
objective: Use scipy.stats for distributions and hypothesis testing.
exercise: Sample 1000 values from N(5,2). Compute mean, std, and plot histogram vs PDF. Compute the CDF probability of x < 6 for N(5,2).
homework: Explain the difference between PDF and CDF. Draw a rough sketch of each.

title: SciPy: Hypothesis Testing
objective: Perform t-tests and chi-square tests from Python.
exercise: Test if the mean of a sample is significantly different from a hypothesized value. Compare means of two groups with an independent t-test. Interpret the p-value.
homework: What does a p-value of 0.03 actually mean? Write the explanation you would give to a non-statistician. Tuesday

title: SciPy: Optimization
objective: Find minima, maxima, and roots of functions numerically.
exercise: Find the minimum of f(x) = x^2 - 4x + 3. Verify analytically. Find the root of x^3 - 2x - 5 = 0 numerically.
homework: Give 3 examples of real-world optimization problems that could be solved with scipy.optimize.

title: SciPy: Integration & Interpolation
objective: Numerically integrate functions and interpolate between data points.
exercise: Numerically integrate sin(x) from 0 to π. Compare with exact answer of 2. Interpolate between 10 sparse data points and plot both original and interpolated curve.
homework: When would you use numerical integration instead of symbolic integration? Wednesday

title: SymPy: Symbolic Mathematics
objective: Define and manipulate mathematical symbols and expressions.
concepts: from sympy import symbols, expand, factor, simplify
exercise: Define x and y as symbols. Expand (x+y)^3. Factor x^2 - 5x + 6. Substitute x=2 into an expression and compare with numeric calculation.
homework: What is the difference between symbolic computation and numerical computation? Give 2 examples of when each is preferred.

title: SymPy: Calculus
objective: Perform differentiation and integration symbolically.
exercise: Compute the derivative of x^3 + 2x^2 - 5x + 1. Find critical points. Compute the definite integral of x^2 from 0 to 3 symbolically. Verify with scipy.
homework: Find the symbolic second derivative of sin(x) * exp(x). Simplify the result. Thursday

title: SymPy: Equation Solving
objective: Solve algebraic equations and systems symbolically.
exercise: Solve x^2 - 4 = 0 and x^2 + x - 6 = 0 symbolically. Solve the system: 2x + y = 5, x - y = 1 symbolically. Compare with numpy.linalg.solve.
homework: When would symbolic solving fail or be impractical? What do you do then?

title: Plotting Mathematical Functions
objective: Visualize symbolic and numeric functions together.
concepts: Converting symbolic expression to numpy function with lambdify()
Plotting function and its derivative together
Finding and marking critical points on plots
exercise: Plot f(x) = x^3 - 3x and its derivative f'(x) on the same axes. Mark local min/max. Use lambdify to convert a SymPy expression to a NumPy function for fast evaluation.
homework: How does lambdify bridge the gap between symbolic math and numerical computation? Friday

title: Scientific Computing in Practice
objective: Apply SciPy + SymPy together to a realistic scientific problem.
exercise: Fit an exponential decay model y = A * exp(-k*t) to noisy data using curve_fit. Plot fit vs data. Report A and k with error estimates. Verify the fit result symbolically with SymPy.
homework: What is residual analysis and why is it important when fitting models to data?

title: Mini-Project: Scientific Mini-Report
objective: Produce a complete scientific analysis combining SciPy and SymPy.
concepts: Combining symbolic derivation + numerical solution + visualization
Writing results in a notebook with markdown
exercise: Choose one: (a) model pendulum motion (ODE solve + plot), (b) analyze a real dataset with t-tests + curve fitting, (c) solve and visualize an optimization problem. Include: symbolic derivation, numerical solution, 2 plots, and interpretation. Bonus: derive the analytical solution symbolically and compare with numerical result.
homework: Reflection: Write a 'scientific computing cheat sheet' covering the 10 most useful SciPy/SymPy functions you learned.

title: Descriptive Statistics Deep Dive
objective: Compute and interpret full descriptive statistics with pandas and NumPy.
concepts: Mean, median, mode: when to use each
exercise: For a real dataset, compute mean/median/mode for 3 numeric columns. Where do they diverge? Identify outliers using the IQR rule (1.5*IQR above Q3 or below Q1).
homework: When is the median a better measure of central tendency than the mean? Give 3 examples.

title: Distributions in Practice
objective: Recognize and simulate common probability distributions.
concepts: Central Limit Theorem: means of samples approach normal
exercise: Simulate samples from 3 distributions. Plot histogram vs theoretical PDF for each. Demonstrate CLT: take means of n=5, 30, 100 samples from a uniform distribution. Show the distribution of means becomes normal.
homework: Explain the Central Limit Theorem in plain language. Why does it matter for data science? Tuesday

title: Correlation & Covariance
objective: Measure and visualize relationships between numeric variables.
concepts: Interpreting correlation coefficients: strength and direction
exercise: Compute correlation matrix for a dataset. Identify 3 strongest and 3 weakest correlations. Create a heatmap. Pick one pair and discuss whether correlation implies causation.
homework: Find a famous example of 'correlation ≠ causation'. Write a 100-word explanation.

title: Hypothesis Testing: t-tests
objective: Test statistical hypotheses using t-tests in Python.
concepts: p-value, alpha level, Type I and II errors
exercise: One-sample test: is mean height of a sample significantly different from 170cm? Two-sample test: do male and female passengers on Titanic have different mean fares? Interpret p-value.
homework: Explain Type I and Type II errors using a medical testing analogy. Which is worse for a cancer screening test? Wednesday

title: ANOVA & Chi-Square Tests
objective: Compare multiple groups and test categorical relationships.
concepts: When to use ANOVA vs t-test
exercise: Test if Iris petal_length differs significantly across all 3 species using ANOVA. Chi-square test: is passenger class independent of survival in Titanic?
homework: When is ANOVA more appropriate than running multiple t-tests? What is the multiple comparisons problem?

title: Confidence Intervals
objective: Estimate population parameters with confidence intervals.
exercise: Compute 95% CI for the mean of a column in a real dataset. Bootstrap: resample 1000 times, compute mean each time. Plot distribution. Extract 2.5th and 97.5th percentiles.
homework: What does '95% confidence interval' actually mean? Write the statistically correct interpretation. Thursday

title: Effect Sizes & Practical Significance
objective: Move beyond p-values to measure the magnitude of effects.
exercise: Compute Cohen's d for a two-group comparison. Interpret: small/medium/large effect. Compare two scenarios: large sample, small effect vs small sample, large effect. Discuss which is more meaningful.
homework: Why can a tiny p-value accompany a practically unimportant effect? Illustrate with numbers.

title: A/B Testing Fundamentals
objective: Apply statistics to real-world A/B testing scenarios.
exercise: Simulate an A/B test: variant A converts at 10%, B at 12%. How large a sample do you need to detect this at α=0.05? Run the test with simulated data and report whether the result is significant.
homework: What is p-hacking? How can you design an experiment to avoid it? Friday

title: Statistical Reporting & Visualization
objective: Communicate statistical results clearly and accurately.
concepts: Error bars on plots (mean ± SE or CI)
exercise: Recreate a publication-style bar chart with error bars (95% CI) for group means. Identify 3 misleading statistical visualizations online and explain what makes them misleading.
homework: What are the 5 most common ways statistics are misused or misrepresented? Write a brief guide.

title: Mini-Project: Stats Story on a Dataset
objective: Answer a real question using end-to-end statistical analysis.
concepts: Question formulation → data selection → EDA → testing → reporting
Combining descriptive stats, plots, hypothesis tests, and effect sizes
exercise: Choose a question: e.g., 'Do weekends have higher restaurant tips?', 'Does class affect Titanic survival odds?'. Perform: EDA (3 plots), hypothesis test with p-value and effect size, confidence interval, written conclusion. Write a 200-word 'findings' section as if presenting to a non-technical stakeholder.
homework: Reflection: Write your 'statistical analysis checklist' — 8 steps you'll always follow when answering a data question.

title: Text Preprocessing & Tokenization
objective: Clean and tokenize raw text for NLP tasks.
exercise: Tokenize a paragraph. Print top 20 word frequencies before and after stopword removal. Write a function clean_text(text) that lowercases, removes punctuation, and strips stopwords.
homework: Why do stopwords matter? Are there cases where you should NOT remove them?

title: Stemming & Lemmatization
objective: Reduce words to their base forms.
exercise: Compare stemming vs lemmatization for: running, flies, better, geese, studies. Write a function that lemmatizes a sentence using POS tags.
homework: Describe a scenario where stemming would produce incorrect results but lemmatization would not. Tuesday

title: Bag-of-Words & TF-IDF
objective: Convert text into numeric features for machine learning.
exercise: Vectorize 5 example sentences with CountVectorizer. Print feature names and matrix. Compare top TF-IDF words for 2 short documents about different topics.
homework: Why does TF-IDF often work better than raw word counts for classification? Explain intuitively.

title: Text Classification with scikit-learn
objective: Build a supervised text classifier.
concepts: Naive Bayes, Logistic Regression for text
exercise: Build a spam vs ham classifier on a small labelled dataset (e.g., SMS spam corpus). Compare Naive Bayes vs Logistic Regression accuracy.
homework: Why is Naive Bayes surprisingly effective for text classification despite its naive assumption? Wednesday

title: spaCy: Pipelines & POS Tagging
objective: Use spaCy for industrial-strength NLP.
concepts: pip install spacy + python -m spacy download en_core_web_sm
exercise: Parse a paragraph with spaCy. List all nouns, all verbs, and all proper nouns. Compare spaCy's tokenization with NLTK's for the same sentence.
homework: What is a dependency tree? Draw the dependency tree for 'The cat sat on the mat'.

title: spaCy: Named Entity Recognition
objective: Identify and classify named entities in text.
concepts: Entity labels: PERSON, ORG, GPE, DATE, MONEY
Custom NER: adding rules with EntityRuler
exercise: Run NER on a news paragraph. List all PERSON and ORG entities. Visualize the entities in a sentence using displacy.render().
homework: List 5 practical applications where named entity recognition is essential. Thursday

title: Word Embeddings: Word2Vec & GloVe Concepts
objective: Understand dense vector representations of words.
concepts: Word embeddings: similar words → close vectors
Using pre-trained embeddings via gensim or spaCy
exercise: Use spaCy's medium model to find 5 most similar words to 'king', 'doctor', 'python'. Compute cosine similarity between 'cat'/'dog' and 'cat'/'car' vectors.
homework: Explain the famous word2vec analogy: king - man + woman ≈ queen. What does this reveal about embeddings?

title: Hugging Face Transformers: Intro
objective: Load and use pre-trained transformer models for NLP tasks.
concepts: Sentiment analysis, text classification, NER with transformers
exercise: Use pipeline('sentiment-analysis') to classify 5 sentences. Examine confidence scores. Use pipeline('ner') to extract entities from a news headline.
homework: What is the difference between BERT and GPT in terms of pre-training objective? Explain in simple terms. Friday

title: Fine-tuning Concepts & Zero-Shot Classification
objective: Understand fine-tuning and use zero-shot classification.
concepts: What fine-tuning means: adjusting pre-trained weights on new data
Zero-shot classification: no training examples needed
exercise: Use zero-shot classification to categorize 10 news headlines into: politics, sports, technology, entertainment. Compare zero-shot results to a simple keyword-based classifier you write manually.
homework: When would zero-shot classification be preferred over training a classifier? When is it not enough?

title: Mini-Project: Sentiment Analysis Tool
objective: Build a complete NLP pipeline from raw text to predictions.
concepts: Combining preprocessing + feature extraction + model + evaluation
Comparing traditional ML vs transformer approach
exercise: Build a sentiment analysis pipeline: (a) with TF-IDF + Logistic Regression, (b) with Hugging Face pipeline. Compare accuracy on a labelled dataset (IMDb or similar). Report which is better and why. Wrap the better model into a simple function analyze_sentiment(text) → {'label': ..., 'confidence': ...}.
homework: Reflection: Write a 'NLP workflow cheat sheet' — the 8 steps from raw text to a working model.

title: Images as Arrays
objective: Understand how images are represented as NumPy arrays.
concepts: BGR vs RGB color ordering in OpenCV
Displaying inline in Jupyter with plt.imshow(cv2.cvtColor)
exercise: Load an image, print shape, dtype. Display it in Jupyter correctly (BGR→RGB). Access pixel value at position (100,100). Change it to red.
homework: Why does OpenCV use BGR instead of RGB? What common mistake does this cause?

title: Color Spaces & Grayscale
objective: Convert between color spaces and understand their use.
exercise: Convert an image to grayscale and HSV. Display all 3 versions side by side. Plot the histogram of the red, green, and blue channels separately.
homework: Why is HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value) useful for color-based object detection compared to BGR? Tuesday

title: Geometric Transformations
objective: Resize, crop, rotate, and flip images.
concepts: Cropping via array slicing: img[y1:y2, x1:x2]
exercise: Resize an image to 50% and 200% of original. Display both. Rotate an image 45° around its center without cropping (use warpAffine with full canvas).
homework: What is interpolation in image resizing? Compare INTER_NEAREST vs INTER_CUBIC visually.

title: Drawing & Annotation
objective: Add shapes and text to images programmatically.
concepts: Copying images before annotation to preserve original
exercise: Draw a labeled bounding box (rectangle + text) around a specific region of an image. Create a 'blank canvas' 400×400 image and draw 5 different shapes on it.
homework: What are the 3 parameters you must get right for cv2.putText() to display clearly? Wednesday

title: Blurring & Filtering
objective: Apply smoothing and sharpening filters.
concepts: cv2.GaussianBlur() with kernel size and sigma
exercise: Apply Gaussian blur with 3 different kernel sizes. Show side by side. Write a sharpening filter kernel and apply it to an image.
homework: Why is Gaussian blur used before edge detection? What happens if you skip it?

title: Edge Detection & Thresholding
objective: Detect edges and separate foreground from background.
exercise: Apply Canny edge detection with low/mid/high thresholds. Compare results. Apply Otsu's thresholding to separate object from background.
homework: What do the two threshold parameters in cv2.Canny() control? How would you tune them? Thursday

title: Contours & Object Detection
objective: Find and draw contours for shape analysis.
concepts: Contour properties: area, perimeter, bounding box
exercise: Find all contours in a binary image. Draw the 3 largest ones. Draw bounding boxes around all detected contours with area > 500.
homework: What preprocessing steps (threshold, blur, morph) give the best contour detection results? Experiment.

title: Template Matching
objective: Find a pattern or sub-image within a larger image.
concepts: Drawing bounding box on the match
exercise: Create a small patch from an image and find it using template matching. Draw a rectangle around all matches above a threshold (multi-match).
homework: What are the main limitations of template matching? When would you need a more powerful approach? Friday

title: Video Processing Basics
objective: Read and process video frames with OpenCV.
concepts: Processing frame-by-frame with a while loop
exercise: Read a short video file and print its FPS, frame count, and resolution. Apply grayscale conversion to each frame and save as a new video.
homework: What is the practical difference between working with images vs video streams in real-time processing?

title: Mini-Project: Image Processing Notebook
objective: Build a complete image processing pipeline notebook.
exercise: Choose an image. Apply: (1) grayscale, (2) Gaussian blur, (3) Canny edges, (4) contour detection with bounding boxes, (5) annotation with text labels. Show each step with a subplot. Write a brief description under each image. Bonus: extend to work on a short video clip.
homework: Reflection: Write your 'OpenCV quick reference card' — the 15 most useful functions with one-line descriptions.

title: SQLite Basics with Python
objective: Create databases and tables from Python.
concepts: cursor.execute() with CREATE TABLE, INSERT, SELECT
exercise: Create a database with a 'students' table (id, name, grade). Insert 5 rows. Query all rows and print them.
homework: What is the difference between conn.commit() and conn.close()? What happens if you forget commit()?

title: CRUD Operations with SQLite
objective: Implement full Create-Read-Update-Delete operations.
exercise: Write functions: insert_student(), get_student(id), update_grade(id, grade), delete_student(id). Test all 4 functions.
homework: Why is string formatting SQL queries dangerous (SQL injection)? Show an example attack. Tuesday

title: pandas ↔ SQLite Integration
objective: Move data between pandas DataFrames and SQLite.
concepts: Filtering and aggregating from SQL via pandas
Comparing SQL GROUP BY vs pandas groupby
exercise: Load a CSV into a SQLite table via pandas. Query with pd.read_sql. Write a SQL query with GROUP BY and compare the result to a pandas groupby.
homework: When would you choose SQL aggregations over pandas groupby? Give 2 scenarios for each.

title: SQLAlchemy ORM Basics
objective: Use SQLAlchemy ORM to interact with databases via Python objects.
concepts: from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Column, String, Integer
exercise: Define a Student model with SQLAlchemy. Create the table. Insert 3 records via ORM. Query all students with grade > 80 using ORM filter.
homework: What are the advantages of using an ORM vs raw SQL? What are the disadvantages? Wednesday

title: Building an ETL Pipeline
objective: Extract, Transform, Load data from files into a database.
concepts: ETL pattern: Extract (read file) → Transform (pandas) → Load (to DB)
Error handling in ETL with try/except
exercise: Build a pipeline: read CSV → clean with pandas (drop nulls, fix types) → write to SQLite → query and verify. Add a log entry for each step to a 'etl_log.txt' file.
homework: What is idempotency in ETL? Why does it matter for scheduled pipelines?

title: Dask DataFrames for Large Data
objective: Process larger-than-memory datasets with Dask.
concepts: Operations are same as pandas but lazy
exercise: Load a large CSV with Dask. Compute groupby mean — compare speed/behavior vs pandas. Use dask to filter and compute statistics on a file too large to fit in memory (simulate with a large file).
homework: When should you use Dask instead of pandas? List 3 scenarios. Thursday

title: Database Design Basics
objective: Design normalized relational database schemas.
exercise: Design a schema for: students, courses, and enrollments. Write the CREATE TABLE SQL for all 3. Insert sample data and write a JOIN query to list students with their courses.
homework: What is the N+1 query problem in databases? How do JOINs solve it?

title: SQL Aggregations & Window Functions
objective: Write advanced SQL queries for analytics.
concepts: Aggregate functions: COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX
Window functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK, LAG, LEAD
exercise: Write a query using GROUP BY + HAVING to find courses with more than 2 enrolled students. Use a window function to rank students by grade within each course.
homework: Explain the difference between WHERE and HAVING. When can each be used? Friday

title: Scheduling & Automation Concepts
objective: Understand how ETL pipelines are scheduled in practice.
concepts: Python schedule library for simple scheduling
Running a script repeatedly with time.sleep()
exercise: Use the schedule library to run a data update function every 60 seconds for a demo. Add a 'last_run.txt' checkpoint so the script skips records already processed.
homework: Research one real-world pipeline orchestration tool (Airflow, Prefect, or Dagster). Write a 100-word summary of what it does.

title: Mini-Project: Mini Analytics Database
objective: Build a complete ETL + analytics pipeline.
concepts: Combining file I/O, pandas, SQLite, and visualization
Full data lifecycle in one project
exercise: Pipeline: load a CSV → clean with pandas → store in SQLite → run 3 analysis queries (groupby, filter, aggregate) → visualize one result as a bar chart → write findings to a markdown summary. Bonus: schedule the pipeline to re-run on new data files.
homework: Reflection: Sketch the architecture of a real production data pipeline you could build with skills from this week. Label each component.

title: Filesystem Automation: os & pathlib
objective: Automate file and directory operations from Python.
exercise: Write a script that organizes files in a folder into subfolders by extension (.pdf/, .csv/, .jpg/ etc.). List all .csv files in a directory tree recursively with pathlib.
homework: Why is pathlib preferred over os.path for new code? List 3 practical advantages.

title: subprocess: Running System Commands
objective: Execute shell commands and capture output from Python.
concepts: Security: never pass user input to shell=True
exercise: Run 'ls' or 'dir' from Python and print the output. Run 'ping' or 'nslookup' and capture stdout to a file.
homework: What is the security risk of subprocess.run('command ' + user_input, shell=True)? How do you avoid it? Tuesday

title: Logging Best Practices
objective: Use Python's logging module for professional script output.
concepts: Log levels: DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL
Logging to file and console simultaneously
Log formatting with timestamps and filenames
exercise: Add logging to yesterday's file organizer script. Log every file operation at INFO level and every error at ERROR level. Set up a logger that writes to both console and 'app.log' file.
homework: Why is print() inadequate for production scripts? List 4 advantages of logging.

title: Exception Handling in Scripts
objective: Write robust scripts that handle errors gracefully.
exercise: Wrap a file reading function with try/except to handle FileNotFoundError and PermissionError separately. Write a custom InvalidDataError exception and raise it in a data validation function.
homework: Describe the difference between finally and else blocks in a try statement. Wednesday

title: Selenium: Browser Automation
objective: Automate browser interactions with Selenium.
exercise: Open a browser with Selenium, navigate to a URL, and take a screenshot. Find and click a button on a webpage programmatically.
homework: What are 3 legitimate use cases for Selenium and 3 uses that would be unethical or against ToS?

title: Selenium: Form Filling & Data Extraction
objective: Use Selenium to interact with forms and dynamic content.
concepts: select element handling with Select class
Extracting text from elements after JavaScript renders
exercise: Automate filling in a search form, submitting, and extracting the first 5 results. Loop through multiple pages of results and collect all titles.
homework: When is Selenium necessary vs BeautifulSoup? What does 'dynamic content' mean in this context? Thursday

title: Testing with pytest
objective: Write and run automated tests for your Python functions.
concepts: Running tests: pytest from command line
exercise: Write 5 tests for your compute_bmi() function (normal cases, edge cases, invalid input). Create a conftest.py fixture that provides a sample DataFrame for multiple tests.
homework: Why should you write tests BEFORE you think your code is working? What is TDD?

title: Building a Reusable Automation Script
objective: Create a complete, well-structured, reusable automation script.
exercise: Build a script that: scans a folder for CSVs, merges them into one, cleans nulls, exports merged CSV. Accept folder and output paths as CLI arguments. Add logging, error handling, and test 3 edge cases.
homework: Run your script from the command line with --help and verify the documentation is clear. Friday

title: Scheduling & Daily Report Script
objective: Combine automation skills into a scheduled reporting script.
concepts: schedule library for simple task scheduling
Combining pandas + report generation + file output
exercise: Create a 'daily_report.py' that reads a CSV, computes summary stats, writes a markdown report to 'reports/report_YYYY-MM-DD.md', and can be scheduled with schedule or cron. Add a test: verify the report file is created with correct content.
homework: Describe the full architecture of a daily automated reporting system for a business. What components would it need?

title: Mini-Project: Full Automation Script
objective: Build and test a complete automation script from scratch.
concepts: Combining filesystem, subprocess, pandas, logging, testing
exercise: Build a complete tool of your choice: e.g., a 'CSV merger and summarizer', a 'file organizer with logging', or a 'website monitor that checks status and logs results'. Includes: CLI args, logging to file, error handling, and at least 5 pytest tests. Write a README.md explaining what it does and how to run it.
homework: Reflection: List the 8 best practices for writing production-quality Python scripts that you learned this week.

title: Capstone: Project Selection & Scoping
objective: Choose a meaningful project and write a clear project plan.
concepts: Project archetypes: data explorer, ML predictor, NLP analyzer, automation tool
Identifying data sources: API, CSV, scraping, open datasets
exercise: Choose your project from 3 options: (a) Weather Data Explorer (API + pandas + plots), (b) House Price Predictor (CSV + pandas + scikit-learn + Flask API), (c) News Sentiment Monitor (API + NLP + visualization). Write a 1-page plan: problem, data source, processing steps, libraries, expected output. Review your plan against this checklist: Is it achievable in 2 weeks? Is the data accessible? Does it use 3+ libraries?
homework: Read your plan aloud. Find 3 potential risks or blockers and write mitigation plans for each.

title: Capstone: Data Acquisition
objective: Collect and store the raw data for your project.
concepts: Fetching data from API or loading from file
Saving raw data as a baseline (never overwrite raw)
Validating data quality immediately after loading
Documenting data source and access date
exercise: Implement data acquisition for your project. Save raw data to 'data/raw/'. Print: shape, dtypes, head(3), missing value counts. Write a data_description.md noting source, date retrieved, rows/columns, known limitations.
homework: What assumptions are you making about the data? List 5 and note which ones you'll need to verify. Tuesday

title: Capstone: Data Cleaning & EDA
objective: Clean raw data and explore key patterns.
concepts: EDA with pandas + Seaborn: 3–5 key plots
exercise: Clean your raw data: fix types, handle missing values, remove duplicates, normalize text if needed. Save cleaned CSV. Create 4 EDA plots that reveal key patterns. Write 1-sentence insight under each.
homework: Write a 'cleaning log' that lists every decision made: what was removed/changed and why.

title: Capstone: Feature Engineering
objective: Create meaningful features from raw data for analysis or modeling.
concepts: Deriving new columns from existing ones
Creating time-based features from datetime columns
exercise: Engineer at least 3 new features for your project. Justify each feature: why should it help? Run a quick correlation analysis: do your new features correlate with the target variable?
homework: Feature engineering is often more impactful than model selection. Describe 2 domain-specific features that could improve your project significantly. Wednesday

title: Capstone: Core Analysis or Model
objective: Implement the central analytical or predictive component.
concepts: For ML projects: Pipeline + cross-validation + metric reporting
For stats projects: hypothesis tests + effect sizes + CIs
For NLP projects: vectorization + classifier or transformer
For data explorer: aggregations + interactive visualization
exercise: Implement your project's core functionality. For ML: train 2 models, report metrics. For analysis: answer your main question with stats. For NLP: build and evaluate classifier. Write 3 'interim findings' cells in your notebook.
homework: What would invalidate your results? List 3 threats to validity and how you're addressing them.

title: Capstone: Visualization & Insights
objective: Create a compelling visual narrative from your results.
concepts: Choosing the right charts for your findings
Building a dashboard concept (optional: Plotly Dash or Flask)
Narrative flow: data → analysis → insight → recommendation
exercise: Create 5 final-quality visualizations for your project. Each should directly support a finding. Write a 'findings summary' section: 3–5 bullet points with supporting numbers.
homework: Show your visualizations to someone unfamiliar with the project. What questions did they ask? What confused them? Update accordingly. Thursday

title: Capstone: API or Interface (Optional)
objective: Expose your project as an API or simple web interface.
concepts: Flask data dashboard with live queries
Input validation and error handling at the interface layer
exercise: If ML project: wrap your best model as a FastAPI /predict endpoint with Pydantic validation. If data project: add a Flask /summary route that returns key stats as JSON. Test every endpoint with both valid and invalid inputs.
homework: What additional features would make your API or interface production-ready? List 5.

title: Capstone: Refactoring & Modularization
objective: Restructure your code into clean, reusable modules.
concepts: Splitting notebook into .py modules: data_loader.py, model.py, viz.py
Using functions for every repeated operation
Creating a main.py or app.py entry point
exercise: Refactor your project: move all functions to at least 2 separate .py files. Ensure your notebook can import and use them. Add docstrings to every function (what it does, args, returns).
homework: Why is modular code better than a single long notebook? List 4 specific benefits. Friday

title: Capstone: Testing & Validation
objective: Write tests for your project's critical components.
concepts: Unit tests for data cleaning functions
Validating model predictions on known inputs
exercise: Write at least 5 pytest tests for your project. Test: data loading, a cleaning function, a feature engineering function, and at least one model prediction. Run pytest and fix any failures.
homework: What would happen if your data source changes format? How would tests catch this? Write a test that would fail on a schema change.

title: Capstone: End-of-Week Review
objective: Review progress against project plan and prepare for final week.
concepts: Identifying what's done, what's missing, what changed
Planning final polish tasks for Week 16
exercise: Complete a project status checklist: data ✓/✗, cleaning ✓/✗, analysis ✓/✗, visualization ✓/✗, API ✓/✗, tests ✓/✗, documentation ✓/✗. Write a 'Week 15 retrospective': what went well, what was harder than expected, what you'd do differently.
homework: Prioritize your Week 16 tasks. Rank them by impact and write your Week 16 action plan.

title: Project Structure & Packaging
objective: Organize your project as a professional Python package.
concepts: Recommended project layout: src/, tests/, data/, notebooks/, docs/
exercise: Reorganize your project into the standard structure. Verify all imports still work. Generate requirements.txt and test that the project runs in a fresh virtual environment.
homework: What is the difference between requirements.txt and Pipfile? When would you use each?

title: Virtual Environments & Reproducibility
objective: Ensure your project runs identically on any machine.
concepts: venv: python -m venv env, activate, deactivate
Testing reproducibility: clone to new folder, install, run
exercise: Create a fresh virtual environment from your requirements.txt. Run the project end-to-end and verify correct output. Identify any version conflicts or missing dependencies and fix them.
homework: What problems arise if you don't pin versions in requirements.txt? Give a real scenario. Tuesday

title: Writing a Professional README
objective: Document your project so others can understand and run it.
concepts: README.md sections: title, description, demo, installation, usage, examples, limitations
Markdown formatting: headers, code blocks, tables, badges
exercise: Write a complete README.md for your project with all sections. Include at least one code example and one screenshot/output sample. Review someone else's GitHub README and identify what makes it good or bad.
homework: Ask a friend or colleague to follow your README from scratch. Note every point where they got confused.

title: Code Documentation & Docstrings
objective: Add comprehensive docstrings and inline comments.
concepts: Inline comments: when they help vs when they clutter
Type hints: def func(x: float) -> str:
exercise: Add complete docstrings to every function in your project (what it does, parameters, returns, raises). Add type hints to at least 5 functions.
homework: What is the difference between a docstring and an inline comment? Give an example of each used correctly. Wednesday

title: Final Testing & Code Review
objective: Ensure tests pass, code is clean, and edge cases are handled.
concepts: Running full test suite with pytest -v
Addressing test failures and edge cases
exercise: Run pytest with coverage report. Aim for >70% coverage on core modules. Fix any test failures. Add 2 more tests for edge cases you hadn't considered.
homework: Review your own code using this checklist: functions < 20 lines? Descriptive names? No magic numbers? Every function has a test?

title: Error Handling & Graceful Degradation
objective: Make your project robust against bad inputs and missing data.
concepts: Catching all expected errors at entry points
Informative error messages vs cryptic tracebacks
Fallback behavior when data is missing
exercise: Deliberately pass bad input to your API or functions. Verify every error is caught and returns a clear message. Add a 'health check' endpoint or function that verifies all dependencies are available.
homework: List 5 ways your project could fail in production. For each, describe what happens now and what should happen. Thursday

title: Deployment Concepts (Intro)
objective: Understand how Python projects are deployed in practice.
exercise: Run your Flask/FastAPI app with uvicorn instead of the dev server. Test that it works. Add a .env file for configuration (API keys, database path). Load with python-dotenv.
homework: Research Docker and write a 150-word summary of: what it is, why it's useful for Python projects, and one limitation.

title: Demo Preparation
objective: Prepare a clear, confident demonstration of your project.
concepts: Demo structure: problem → data → solution → results → demo
Live demo vs recorded demo trade-offs
Telling a data story: data → insight → action
exercise: Write a demo script: 5-minute walkthrough covering problem, approach, 3 key findings, and live demo or output. Rehearse once and note where you stumble or where explanations were unclear.
homework: Write down the 3 questions you most expect from your audience and prepare clear, concise answers. Friday

title: Final Demo & Presentation
objective: Present your completed project confidently.
concepts: Communicating technical work to a mixed audience
Connecting your work to real-world impact
exercise: Deliver your 5-minute demo (live or recorded). Focus on: what question you answered, how you answered it, what you found, what someone could do with this. After the demo: list 5 things you would add or improve if you had 2 more weeks.
homework: Write a 'project one-pager': problem, approach, key finding, and next steps — as if sending it to a manager or client.

title: Course Retrospective & Next Steps
objective: Reflect on 16 weeks of learning and plan your continued growth.
exercise: Complete a self-assessment matrix: rate yourself (1–5) on every library covered. Identify your top 3 strengths and top 3 gaps. Write a 'Learning Roadmap': the 3 most important things you'll learn next, with specific resources and a timeline. Bonus: Push your capstone project to GitHub with a complete README. This is your portfolio piece.
homework: Final reflection: Write 5 'letters to your Week 1 self' — advice you wish you'd had at the beginning. Share them with future students. Appendix: Reusable Jupyter Notebook Template Copy this template for every 30-minute block. Replace the bracketed placeholders with the specific week, day, block number, and topic. Notebook Cell Layout # [WkX-DayY-BlockZ] Topic: <Short title here> ## 1. Objective - What will I learn or practice in this 30-minute block? - Link to weekly theme: e.g., Week 3 – Pandas basics ## 2. Setup ```python import numpy as np        # if needed import pandas as pd       # if needed import matplotlib.pyplot as plt  # if needed # %matplotlib inline ``` ## 3. Warm-up / Recap (5 minutes) Briefly restate one concept from the previous block. ## 4. Main Concept / Example (10–15 minutes) ### 4.1 Concept Summary - Key concept in 1–3 bullet points - Key methods/functions: method1, method2, method3 ### 4.2 Code Examples ```python # Example 1: <short description> # TODO: Write code here # Example 2: <short description> # TODO: Write code here ``` ## 5. Exercise(s) (10–15 minutes) **Exercise 1** Description: <What to implement> TODOs: - [ ] Step 1: ... - [ ] Step 2: ... - [ ] Step 3: ... ```python # Your solution here ``` ## 6. Quick Check / Self-Evaluation - [ ] I can explain what <concept> does in my own words - [ ] I can use <method1> and <method2> without looking at docs - [ ] I ran all cells and got expected outputs ## 7. Notes and Questions New things I learned: ... Things I found confusing: ... Questions to revisit next block: ...
